name: chile colorado
instructions: rehydrate the guajillo and california chiles. saute yellow onions and garlic, then blend the sauteed onions, garlic, and rehydrated chiles together into a smooth paste. fry the paste in a pan, then simmer with the meat (or beans) in bouillon. for chili powder, new mexico chili powder is best — NOT paprika/cumin blends. add masa harina near the end to thicken the sauce. liquid largely cooks off; the goal is a thick red sauce coating meat or beans, not a soupy stew.
notes:
INTRO: this is not traditional chile colorado — traditional chile colorado uses real beef (often chuck) slow-cooked for hours. we use ground beef or vegan ground beef and skip the long braise.

BLENDED ONIONS: blending the onions smooth with the chiles is important. if you leave the onions diced (and especially if you add tomato), you end up with something closer to american chili — a different recipe entirely (diced peppers, onions, tomato, simmered as a stew). chile colorado is about a smooth chile-onion-garlic paste fried down into a thick sauce.

DRIED CHILES (for the paste): california and guajillo only. ancho and pasilla are traditional in many chile colorado recipes but i don't like them as much here — they pull the sauce darker and more bitter than i want.

JALAPENO: jalapeno adds depth in much the same way worcestershire does — it's not really there for the heat, it's there for the savory backbone the sauce otherwise lacks (especially in the meat-light versions). especially useful for brightening up a bean-only version of the dish, where the lack of meat leaves the sauce flatter and the jalapeno's vegetal sharpness fills in for what would otherwise be coming from the beef. anaheim peppers can ride alongside as a milder fresh-pepper note.

CHICKEN: chicken is not good in the final dish (which should be composed of only solid beans or dark/red meat). but chicken thighs can be cooked in the broth and then REMOVED to make a chicken side dish coated in the red sauce, while also flavoring the rest of the chili. don't leave the chicken in the final pot.

BEANS: pinto is the canonical bean. white beans are fine alongside pinto, but never use white beans instead of pinto — pinto carries the dish.

NO TOMATO: tomato in any form (fresh, canned, paste) is incorrect for chile colorado. that's american chili.

WORCESTERSHIRE / NUTRITIONAL YEAST: both add depth and tang the dish lacks when there's no real beef doing umami work (i.e. the vegetarian / vegan-ground-beef versions). using them does pull the dish slightly toward american chili, but as a correction step for the no-real-meat version it's worth the drift — better than a flat sauce.

FAILURE MODE — TOO MUCH BOUILLON IN A BEAN CHILI: bean versions of this dish reduce much further than meat-stew versions because the beans absorb a lot of liquid. easy mistake path: while cooking chicken or meat in the broth, the broth tastes bland (or pungent from the raw onions and blended peppers), so you add more bouillon to push savoriness. by the time the chicken comes out and the beans go in, that extra bouillon over-concentrates as the liquid reduces and ends up fighting you — the final dish reads salty and metallic rather than rich.

Fix: don't fix early-stage broth blandness with more bouillon. instead, lean on green peppers (jalapenos especially, anaheim alongside) to brighten and balance the early broth's stewiness. you still want the onions blended smooth (not chunky) — that part of the technique doesn't change — but adding some fresh pepper character early on lifts the broth without committing you to extra salt that will haunt the bean phase.
